And the issue was that some of the keywords that they found, and I kind of was like, okay, I assumed that they were able to do this in a way that was like, would get us good topics.
What they didn't understand was the concept of related keywords, and that sometimes, okay, two keywords can be different technically.
But when you Google them, the search results are the same, which means that those keywords are the same.
So if we target those keywords.
With different types of content, we are creating duplicate content for the client.
We're going to dilute their results.
And overall, our results just aren't going to be impressive because we're just going to have all sorts of content on their site that isn't impactful.
So that's really where the danger is.
Maybe the client won't notice at the time of us suggesting those topics that we shouldn't be doing those topics.
However, if there's an SEO expert on the other end, they could be like, well, I don't think this is good.
These are all synonymous keywords.
Why are we doing this?
So that's why I feel very strongly that engagement managers need to be able to tell what's a good topic versus what's not a good topic and how are we prioritizing things.

This is a sample report.
So the AI found a fake report that it cited, but it didn't know that it was fake, and we didn't do the checking.
So I saw that.
was like, we need to remove this.

This is why I'm so hesitant to really let CMs kind of deliver the work, because this is really, like, what won't make us look good.
And then.
That would kind of be P0, I think.
P1 would be just not sanity checking the AI.
So like I said in the sprint stand-up today, we don't really question the AI, and the AI loves to make absolute statements with a lot of confidence.
We need to be able to question that and be like, okay, well, does this actually make sense?
Like today I was editing an article, and we kept saying like, if the data in your CRM and billing systems don't match, then that means one of the numbers is wrong.
It's like, well, duh, like obviously.
I mean, what about it?
Like what is the main message that we're trying to say?
Like you have an issue with like data discrepancies, like data silos, like what are we trying to say?
So these are the types of things like we need to really watch out for.
Things like flow, transitions, I feel like that's probably like P2.
This is something we...
We can refine.

And that's fine.
So I think those are the things where we could, you know, kind of iterate with the client and it's not going to harm the relationship.
But, you know, links, stats, source verification, that's like the number one priority.
The second one would be kind of making sure that the AI isn't making very confident statements that don't make no sense.
